answer:
**Chapter 42 ("De l’influence exercée sur quelques-uns des peuples d’Europe, par les circonstances locales au milieu desquelles ils ont été placés"), in which Comte examines how local circumstances—including terrain and climate—influence the progress of various European peoples (contrasting, e.g., favorable terrains like those near the Mediterranean with unfavorable ones like Lapland).**[^1]

There, he argues that terrain and climate determine the kinds of productive activities (especially agriculture and related arts) that peoples can pursue, as they dictate what plants can grow and thus what knowledge and industries develop:

> In Europe as in Asia, there are countries that are not susceptible to producing any kind of plants proper for human subsistence [...]. But, between a country that produces nothing, and one where almost all the productions of the earth can grow, there are a great number of intermediate stages [...]. One can thus conceive that **knowledge relative to agriculture, and to the numerous arts connected with it, extends as one passes from a terrain that is not susceptible to being cultivated, like Lapland, to a terrain on which the most varied and most useful productions can grow**.[^2]

Comte reinforces this throughout the chapter (e.g., via England's coal-rich terrain enabling manufacturing industry, or Russia's poor communications and cold climate hindering it), tying terrain/climate to the "means of transport," "agricultural productions," and overall "industry" that shape a people's development.[^3]

Chapters 30–32 and 39–41 analogously link "moyens d’existence" (means of subsistence/productive activity) for Caucasian ("European stock") peoples—including Arabs in hot/desert terrains (pastoralism) and those transplanted outside Europe—to climate/terrain, but 42 most directly theorizes the mechanism for Europeans proper.[^4]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [Chapter 42](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/42-de-l-influence-exercee-sur-quelques-uns-des-peuple/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/42-de-l-influence-exercee-sur-quelques-uns-des-peuple/translation/#p-be40f2).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [paragraphs](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/42-de-l-influence-exercee-sur-quelques-uns-des-peuple/translation/#p-f9e941), e.g., "The nature of the soil and the temperature of the atmosphere have, on all agricultural productions, an influence..."; see also [Chapter 42 citation_url](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/42-de-l-influence-exercee-sur-quelques-uns-des-peuple/translation/#p-ce851d) on England's coal/terrain enabling industry.

[^4]: E.g., Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL III*, [Chapter 30](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/30-des-rapports-observes-entre-les-moyens-d-existence/translation/#p-378def) (Bedouins' pastoralism from desert terrain); [Chapter 39](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a/39-de-l-influence-exercee-sur-les-peuples-d-afrique-d/translation/#p-a7fc0b) (sandy terrains limiting activity). Chapter 50 discusses Europeans' success *outside* Europe but attributes it more to imported improvements than local climate/terrain shaping their activity.

args.query: "terrain" OR "soil" OR "land" "means of existence" OR "mode of subsistence" OR "productive activity" OR "industry"
"europeans" "america" OR "africa" OR "asia" OR "india" OR "colonies" climate OR terrain
